24: shivim shavuim nechetach al-amecha vehal ir qadshecha lekale happesha ulehatem chataut velchapper avon ulehavi tsdeq olamim velachtom chazot venavia velimshoach qodesh qodashim: 'Seventy weeks are decreed about your people and your holy city, to finish the transgression, to put an end to sin, to atone for iniquity, to bring in everlasting righteousness, to seal both vision and prophet, and to anoint a most holy place.' The six infinitives of Dan 9:24 have generated centuries of calculation and debate. The shavuim (weeks/sevens) are most naturally weeks of years (seven-year units), giving 490 years from the decree to rebuild Jerusalem. The six goals — which are systematically soteriological and eschatological — align most naturally with Christ's work: atonement (to finish transgression, atone for iniquity), righteousness (bring in everlasting righteousness), and the end of the prophetic age (seal vision and prophet).
1: bishnat achat leDaryavesh ben Achashverosh mizzera Madai (bišnat ʾaḥat lĕdāryāwēš ben ʾăḥašwērôš mizzeraʿ māday): 'In the first year of Darius son of Ahasuerus, of Median descent.' The dating formula is precise: Darius (identity debated — either a secondary title for Cyrus or an otherwise unrecorded official) is identified by parentage and ethnicity. Mizzera Madai (of Median seed) situates Daniel's prayer in the Medo-Persian transition, the moment when the seventy-year prophecy of Jeremiah (9:2) is approaching fulfillment. The historical precision grounds the prayer in real time — Daniel calculates the prophetic clock.
2: binah b'sefarim (bînāh bĕsĕpārîm): 'understood from the scrolls/books.' Binah (H998) is not merely reading but interpretive understanding — the same root as the heavenly figure's instruction to Daniel to understand (hitbonen, v.23). Sefarim (scrolls) refers to the written prophetic corpus already circulating as authoritative texts; Daniel treats Jeremiah's prophecy as canonical Scripture. Shivim shanah (seventy years): Jer 25:11-12 and 29:10 — Daniel is calculating the prophetic timeline. This is one of the earliest explicit examples of prophetic interpretation of prior prophecy, establishing the tradition of inner-biblical exegesis.
3: etten et-panai el-Adonai ha'Elohim (ʾettēn ʾet-pānay ʾel-ʾădōnāy hāʾĕlōhîm): 'I set my face toward the Lord God.' Natan panim (set the face) is a Hebrew idiom for deliberate, focused orientation — the whole self directed toward God in covenantal address. The prayer disciplines listed — tsom (fasting), saq (sackcloth), efer (ashes) — are the classic triad of covenant mourning, mirroring Jonah 3:6 and Esther 4:1-3. Daniel mourns over the covenant's broken condition before he prays for its restoration, establishing penitence as the necessary posture for effective intercessory prayer.
4: el gadol venora shomer haberit vehahesed (ʾēl gādôl wĕnôrāʾ šômēr habbĕrît wĕhahesed): 'great and awesome God who keeps the covenant and steadfast love.' Daniel's prayer opens with YHWH's covenant-character as the basis for appeal — not human merit but divine attribute. Nora (awesome/fearsome): the same term used in Exod 34:10; Deut 10:21. Shomer haberit vehahesed: the covenant-keeping formula from the Deuteronomic blessing tradition (Deut 7:9, 12), invoked here as the ground of Daniel's hope. Prayer appeals to who God is, not what Israel deserves.
5: chatanu va'avinu hirshanu umradnu vesor mimitzvotecha (ḥāṭāʾnû wĕʿāwînû hirŝaʿnû ûmāradnû wĕsôr mimmitswōtêkā): 'We have sinned and done wrong and acted wickedly and rebelled and turned aside from your commandments.' Daniel deploys five Hebrew sin-verbs in rapid sequence — one of the most comprehensive sin-catalogues in Scripture. Chata (H2398, miss the mark), avah (H5753, twist/pervert), hirshia (H7561, act wickedly/be in the wrong), marad (H4775, rebel openly), sur (H5493, turn aside/deviate). This is not rhetorical accumulation but theological precision: sin is simultaneously moral failure, relational distortion, covenantal rebellion, and directional deviation.
6: velo shamanu el-avadecha haneviim (wĕlōʾ šāmaʿnû ʾel-ʿăbādêkā hannĕbîʾîm): 'We did not listen to your servants the prophets.' Shama (H8085) is simultaneously to hear and to obey — non-listening is non-obedience. The phrase avadecha haneviim (your servants the prophets) is the characteristic Deuteronomistic designation for the prophetic office (2 Kgs 9:7; 17:13, 23; Amos 3:7; Jer 7:25). The chain — YHWH sent, prophets spoke, Israel refused to hear — identifies the theological mechanism of the exile: covenantal deafness to the divine word.
7: lecha Adonai hatsedaqah velanu bosheth hapanim (lĕkā ʾădōnāy haṣṣĕdāqāh wĕlānû bôšet happānîm): 'To you, Lord, belongs righteousness; to us shame of face.' The antithesis is theologically precise: tsedaqah (H6666) here is YHWH's covenant-faithfulness and rectifying justice — the quality by which he acts consistently with his own covenant character. Bosheth hapanim (shame of face) is a Semitic idiom for public disgrace and dishonor. The exile is the visible manifestation of this shame — Israel's covenantal failure exposed before the nations. Daniel prays not by hiding this but by owning it as the starting point for covenantal appeal.
8: lanu bosheth hapanim lamelachim leshareinu uleavoteinu asher chatanu lach: 'To us belongs shame of face — to our kings, our officials, and our fathers who sinned against you.' The scope of the confession is comprehensive: shame belongs to every stratum of Israelite society — monarchy, administration, and the ancestral heritage. Daniel does not exempt the leadership from the communal confession; the exile is a systemic failure, not merely individual lapses. The solidarity of confession — chatanu lach (we sinned against you) — is the model of corporate representative prayer that shapes later Jewish liturgy.
9: laAdonai Eloheinu harachamim vehaselichot ki maradnu bo (laʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhênû hāraḥămîm wĕhasslîḥôt kî māradnû bô): 'To the Lord our God belong mercy and forgiveness, for we have rebelled against him.' Rachamim (H7356, mercies/compassions): plural intensive of rechem (womb) — YHWH's compassion is as intimate and unconditional as a mother's for her child (Isa 49:15). Selichot (H5547, forgivenesses): plural, occurring only three times in the OT (Neh 9:17; Ps 130:4; here) — the plural emphasizes the inexhaustible character of divine forgiveness. The logic is daring: because we have rebelled, YHWH's forgiveness-quality is now the only hope.
10: velo shamanu beqol YHWH Eloheinu lalechet betorotav: 'We did not listen to the voice of YHWH our God by walking in his laws.' The phrase lalechet (to walk) marks Torah-observance as a mode of life — halakha (from halakh, walk) later becomes the rabbinic technical term for the whole body of practical commandments. Not listening to the divine voice is not an abstract theological failure but a behavioral deviation — Israel's feet went the wrong direction. The Torah given through Moses and the subsequent prophets is the road; Israel refused to walk it.
11: nitach aleinu ha'alah (nittaḥ ʿālênû hāʾālāh): 'The curse has been poured out upon us.' Alah (H423) is specifically the covenantal oath-curse — the treaty-curse that YHWH swore would come if Israel broke the covenant (Deut 28:15-68; Lev 26:14-43). The exile is the alah-sanction activated: not random misfortune but the precise covenantal consequence that Moses and the prophets had warned of. Nitach (poured out) — the same pouring-out language used for libation offerings is here applied to curse — the covenant has been poured out in judgment rather than blessing.
12: vayaqem et-devarav asher diber aleinu: 'He confirmed his words that he spoke against us.' YHWH's faithfulness works in two directions: he keeps his covenant promises of blessing (v.4), and he keeps his covenant threats of curse. The exile is proof of YHWH's consistency — his word is reliable even when it falls as judgment. This theological point is important: Israel cannot claim YHWH's promises while denying his warnings. Daniel's prayer acknowledges that YHWH acted justly in bringing the disaster — the prophets' warnings were true.
13: velo challinu et-penei YHWH Eloheinu lashuv meavoneinu: 'We did not entreat the face of YHWH our God, turning from our iniquities.' Challah et-panim (H2470 + H6440, entreat/pacify the face of) is the idiom for seeking divine favor through prayer and repentance. Lashuv (to return/repent): shuv (H7725) is the primary Hebrew repentance term — a turning-of-direction, not merely an internal feeling. The failure was not primarily intellectual (they did not understand the Torah rightly) but volitional: they knew but did not turn. Velo haskilnu be'amitecha (not attending to your truth) — emet as truth/faithfulness is the ground of prayer.
14: vayishkod YHWH al-hara'ah vayevi'iha aleinu: 'YHWH was watchful over the disaster and brought it upon us.' Shaqad (H8245, be watchful/alert) is the same root as the almond (shaqed) imagery in Jer 1:11-12 — YHWH is watching to perform his word. The disaster was not YHWH sleeping or ignoring; he was actively watching to execute his just judgment. Ki-tsaddiq YHWH Eloheinu al-kol-ma'asav asher asah ve'lo shamanu b'qolo: YHWH is righteous (tsaddiq) in all he has done — the exile vindicated rather than indicted YHWH's character.
15: ve'attah Adonai Eloheinu asher hotzeta et-amcha me'eretz Mitsraim beyad chazaqah: 'And now, Lord our God, who brought your people out of Egypt with a mighty hand.' The pivot from confession to petition is bridged by the Exodus appeal — YHWH's paradigmatic act of salvation. Yad chazaqah (mighty hand) is the standard Deuteronomic descriptor for the Exodus (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:8). Just as YHWH delivered from Egypt, so he can deliver from Babylon. The prayer's logic: the same God who acted then can act now, and his name's reputation is at stake in both cases.
16: bechol-tsidqotecha yashuv-na aphcha chamatha: 'In all your acts of righteousness, let your anger and wrath turn away.' Tsidqotecha (your righteousnesses): plural, referring to the accumulation of YHWH's saving acts in Israel's history — each rescue, each covenant renewal is an act of tsedaqah. These past acts are the basis for appealing to future action. The pairing of aph (burning anger) and chamah (wrath/heat) is the standard merism for divine anger — Daniel does not minimize the reality of YHWH's wrath but appeals for its redirection from the city to YHWH's mercy.
17: shema Elohenu el-tefillat avdecha: 'Hear, our God, the prayer of your servant.' Shema at the prayer's climax echoes the Shema of Deut 6:4 — the same verb now turned back to God. Daniel prays that YHWH will hear as he commanded Israel to hear. Veha'er panecha al-miqdashecha hashamem: 'Let your face shine upon your desolated sanctuary.' The sanctuary's desolation is both the problem and the theological test — a desolate temple suggests an absent God. The prayer is that YHWH's luminous presence will return to the destroyed place, reversing the departure of the glory described in Ezekiel 10-11.
18: ki lo al-tsidqoteinu anachnu maphilim tachanunenu lefanecha: 'For it is not on account of our righteousness that we present our pleas before you.' The final ground-clearing: all appeal to human merit is renounced. Tachanunenu (our pleas/supplications) — tachanun from chanan (H2603, show favor/grace) — the prayer itself is an appeal for grace, not a claim of righteousness. Ki al-rachamecha harabim: 'but on account of your great mercies.' The prayer rests entirely on the divine attribute of rachamim rabim (great mercies) — what YHWH is by nature and covenant, not what Israel has earned.
19: Adonai shema Adonai selach Adonai haqshivah va'aseh al-te'acher: 'Lord, hear! Lord, forgive! Lord, listen and act! Do not delay!' The triple invocation of Adonai creates an urgent litany — each address adds a new petition: hearing, forgiveness, attentive action, and urgency. Lemaan shimcha: 'for your own name's sake' — the prayer's ultimate appeal is YHWH's reputation among the nations. Ki shimcha nikra al-ircha ve'al-amecha: your name is called over your city and your people — the name-theology of covenant means YHWH's honor is bound up with the destiny of Jerusalem and Israel.
20: vod ani medabber umitpallel: 'While I was still speaking and praying.' The immediacy of the divine response (Gabriel arrives while Daniel is still speaking, v.21) demonstrates that YHWH hears prayer in real time. Matir chatati vachetat ami Yisrael: 'confessing my sin and the sin of my people Israel' — the intercessory prayer model is Daniel's own sin bound up with the community's. The individual representative mediates for the corporate body — the pattern of all biblical intercession.
21: vehaish Gavriel asher ra'iti bachazoni batchillah muaf biye'af: 'The man Gabriel whom I had seen in the vision at the beginning, flying swiftly.' Gavriel (H1403) = gavar (H1368, mighty/strong) + El (God) — the mighty one of God. This is one of only four named angels in the canonical OT/NT (Gabriel, Michael, Raphael in Tobit, and Lucifer/Satan as adversarial figure). Muaf biye'af: the phrase for flight is debated — either flying with swiftness or approaching in a state of exhaustion/weariness. Either way, Gabriel's arrival is direct divine response to Daniel's prayer.
22: vayiven oti vayedabber immi vayomar Daniel atah yatsati lehaskil binah: 'He made me understand and spoke to me, saying: Daniel, I have now come out to give you insight and understanding.' Lehaskil (to give insight/make wise) and binah (understanding): both from roots associated with wisdom and discernment. Gabriel's mission is not merely information delivery but cognitive enhancement — the revelation opens Daniel's understanding to grasp what mere human reasoning cannot reach. This is the model of biblical revelation: God gives not just data but the interpretive capacity to receive it.
23: bithchillat tachanuneicha yatsa dabar vaani bati lehagid ki chamudot attah: 'At the beginning of your pleas a word went out, and I have come to tell you, for you are greatly beloved.' Chamudot (H2532, beloved/precious/desirable): Daniel is described as ish chamudot (beloved man) in 10:11, 19 as well — a term of divine affection. The timing — the word went out at the beginning of the prayer — indicates that YHWH's response was initiated immediately upon Daniel's prayer, not after waiting for its completion. Intercessory prayer is heard as it begins.
25: umeshiach nagid shavuim shiva uveshavuim shishim ushnayim: 'And from the going out of the word to restore and build Jerusalem to the coming of an anointed prince, there shall be seven weeks. And for sixty-two weeks it shall be built again.' Mashiach nagid (anointed prince): mashiach is the anointed one — used for priests (Lev 4:3), kings (1 Sam 16:6; Ps 2:2), and here the expected eschatological figure. Nagid (H5057, prince/leader/commander) is a royal term. The mathematical structure (7 + 62 + 1 = 70 weeks) has generated centuries of calculation; the referents remain debated. The key theological point: YHWH has decreed a specific redemptive timeline, and the anointed one's coming is the center of the seventy-week period.
26: yikaret mashiach ve'ein lo (yikkārēt māšîaḥ wĕʾên lô): 'The anointed one will be cut off and will have nothing (or: not for himself).' Karet (H3772, cut off/cut down) is the standard Torah term for judicial death and covenant exclusion (used for capital offenses: Exod 31:14; Lev 7:20-21; Num 15:30-31). Ve'ein lo is ambiguous: either the anointed one receives nothing (no kingdom, no inheritance) or the cutting off is not for himself (i.e., not for his own sin — the same construction as Isa 53:8's mipesha ammi nega lamo). The latter reading aligns with Isaiah's Servant dying for others, not for his own transgressions.
27: vehigbir berit larabim shavua echad vachatsit hashavua yashbit zevach umincha: 'He shall make a strong covenant with many for one week, and for half of the week he shall put an end to sacrifice and offering.' Higbir berit (strengthen/confirm covenant): gavar (H1396, be strong/prevail) in Hiphil — to cause the covenant to prevail. Larabim (for the many) — the phrase echoes Isa 53:11-12 (yatsdiq tsaddiq avdi larabim, my righteous servant makes many righteous). Shiquts meshomem (abomination of desolation): shiquts (H8251, detestable thing/idol) + meshomem (H8074, causing desolation/stunned). This phrase (also 11:31; 12:11) becomes Jesus's own term for the end-time crisis (Mark 13:14 and parallels).
